1.synthesis: Brothers, if anyone is caught in any transgression, those who are spiritual should restore him in a spirit of gentleness—each keeping watch on himself lest he too be tempted. Bear one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ (Galatians 6:1–2). If anyone thinks he is something when he is nothing, he deceives himself. Let each test his own work—then his reason to boast will be in himself alone and not in his neighbor. For each will have to bear his own load (Galatians 6:3–5). Let the one who is taught the word share all good things with the one who teaches (Galatians 6:6). Do not be deceived: God is not mocked, for whatever one sows, that will he also reap. The one who sows to his own flesh will from the flesh reap corruption, but the one who sows to the Spirit will from the Spirit reap eternal life. And let them not grow weary in doing good—for in due season they will reap, if they do not give up. So then, as they have opportunity, let them do good to everyone, and especially to those who are of the household of faith (Galatians 6:7–10).
1.historical_context: 'Those who are spiritual' (v.1) does not designate a spiritual elite but those who are walking by the Spirit (5:16–25)—all believers are called to this, and all believers should participate in the restoration of the fallen. The 'law of Christ' (v.2) is Paul's summary of the love-commandment (5:14) as Christ's own teaching and example: bearing one another's burdens is the concrete form that the love-neighbor commandment takes in community life. The apparent tension between 'bear one another's burdens' (v.2, plural baros = heavy load) and 'each will have to bear his own load' (v.5, singular phortion = pack carried by a soldier) is not contradictory: the communal burden-bearing is for the crushing weights that individuals cannot carry alone; the personal load is the individual's own responsibility before God. The sowing-reaping principle (vv.7–8) is the created-order moral logic: actions have consequences that correspond to their character—flesh-sowing produces corruption; Spirit-sowing produces eternal life.
1.literary_structure: The community ethics section (6:1–10) applies the Spirit-fruit of 5:22–26 to specific social situations: the fallen brother restored (vv.1–2), the self-deceived corrected (vv.3–5), the teacher supported (v.6), the principle of sowing and reaping (vv.7–9), and the programmatic doing-good to all (v.10). The 'law of Christ' (v.2) is the letter's ethical summary: the law the Galatians are being pressured to adopt (Mosaic Torah) is displaced by the law of Christ (love-burden-bearing), which is the Spirit's fruit in communal form. The 'household of faith' (v.10) as the primary community of good-doing does not exclude outsiders ('everyone') but prioritizes the community of the Spirit as the primary arena of Christian mutual care.
1.theological_application: 'Bear one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ' (v.2) is the ethical center of the letter's closing section: the 'law of Christ' is not a new legal code but the love-pattern that Christ embodied—the one who bore the weight of human sin and condemnation fulfills the law in himself, and those who bear one another's burdens participate in that fulfillment. Restoration of the fallen 'in a spirit of gentleness' (v.1) is the communal form of the fruit of the Spirit: gentleness (5:23) is the character in which restoration happens—not punitive, not triumphant over the fallen, but gentle and watchful of one's own vulnerability. The sowing-reaping principle (vv.7–8) is not a prosperity-gospel promise but a creation-order moral reality: the shape of one's life (toward flesh or toward Spirit) determines its ultimate fruit. 'Do not grow weary in doing good' (v.9) is the necessary encouragement: the good that love does is often slow, costly, and unrewarded in the short term.
1.christology: 'The law of Christ' (v.2) names the ethical standard of the new community as Christ's own, not Paul's invention: the law that fulfills the Torah's love-demand is the law of the one who himself 'loved me and gave himself for me' (2:20). Burden-bearing in the community is participation in Christ's own burden-bearing: he bore the curse (3:13) so that others might be freed; those who belong to him bear one another's weights so that none are crushed alone. The 'Spirit reaping eternal life' (v.8) is not individual immortality but participation in the resurrection life of the risen Christ—the eternal life that the Spirit gives is the life of Christ shared with those who belong to him. Doing good 'especially to those who are of the household of faith' (v.10) recognizes the community of the Spirit as the primary sphere of the risen Christ's body: the mutual care of believers for one another is the communal form of the body of Christ caring for itself.

11.synthesis: See with what large letters Paul is writing in his own hand (Galatians 6:11). Those who want to make a good showing in the flesh are the ones compelling circumcision—only so that they may not be persecuted for the cross of Christ. For even the circumcised do not themselves keep the law, but want them to be circumcised so that they may boast in their flesh (Galatians 6:12–13). But far be it from Paul to boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, by which the world has been crucified to him, and he to the world (Galatians 6:14). For neither circumcision counts for anything, nor uncircumcision, but a new creation. And as for all who walk by this rule, peace and mercy be upon them, and upon the Israel of God (Galatians 6:15–16). Let no one cause him trouble, for he bears on his body the marks of Jesus. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers. Amen (Galatians 6:17–18).
11.historical_context: The 'large letters' (v.11) of Paul's own handwriting authenticate the letter (secretaries wrote Paul's letters; Paul signed them): either because of poor eyesight (cf. 4:15: 'you would have given your eyes') or for emphasis, Paul writes the closing in his own large script. The agitators' motive is exposed in vv.12–13: they compel circumcision 'to avoid persecution for the cross'—maintaining Jewish social standing in a community where Jewish Christians were under pressure to demonstrate Torah-fidelity. Their boasting is 'in the flesh' (circumcision as social-religious credential); Paul's boasting is 'in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ' (the crucified One whose death ended all credential-boasting). 'The marks of Jesus' (v.17) are Paul's scars from beatings, stonings, and imprisonments—the physical evidence of persecution for the cross that the agitators are trying to avoid. The 'Israel of God' (v.16) is the community of all who walk by the rule of new creation—Jew and Gentile who are Abraham's offspring through faith in Christ.
11.literary_structure: The autograph closing (vv.11–18) is the letter's final restatement of its central thesis: circumcision vs. cross, flesh-boasting vs. cross-boasting, old creation vs. new creation. The structure is chiastic: agitators' motive (avoid cross-persecution, boast in flesh, vv.12–13) ↔ Paul's boast (the cross, vv.14) → new creation as the only criterion (vv.15–16) → Paul's marks of Jesus as his credential (v.17) → grace benediction (v.18). The 'new creation' (v.15) is the letter's ultimate theological category: beyond circumcision and uncircumcision, the new creation in Christ is the eschatological reality that all the letter's arguments have been serving. The final benediction restores the family-language ('brothers') that has appeared throughout the letter as a reminder that Paul's sharpest arguments are in the service of familial love.
11.theological_application: 'Far be it from me to boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, by which the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world' (v.14) is the letter's personal climax: the cross has ended Paul's relationship to the world's system of value—ethnic credential, social status, religious performance, moral achievement. The world's criteria of boasting (in which circumcision is a credential and uncircumcision is a deficit) have been crucified along with the old self (2:20). What remains is the new creation: 'neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts for anything, but a new creation' (v.15). The 'rule' (kanōn) by which peace and mercy is pronounced is not the Mosaic Torah but the new-creation criterion: are you in Christ, walking by the Spirit, bearing the fruit of love? This is the only credential that matters in the community that has been constituted by the cross.
11.christology: 'Boast only in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ' (v.14) is the letter's christological signature: the cross is the only legitimate object of apostolic and Christian boasting because it is the event in which the world's value-system was exposed and ended, in which the curse was absorbed and the blessing released, in which the old age died and the new creation was born. 'The marks of Jesus' (v.17) are Paul's apostolic credential—not letters of recommendation, not circumcision, not rhetorical skill, but the bodily scars of persecution for the cross. These marks are the sign of true apostleship precisely because they conform Paul to the pattern of the crucified Christ whose marks defined his identity in the resurrection (John 20:27). The grace that closes the letter—'the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit'—is the same grace that opened it (1:3) and that has governed every argument: the grace of the Son who gave himself, the grace that neither circumcision can add to nor uncircumcision diminish.
